Gemini said
Checking the parallax effect in real-time is absolutely the right next step. A jittery or lagging hero section instantly kills that "Clinical Luxury" vibe and can severely impact user trust.

However, because I am an AI, I don't have the ability to open your browser, view your screen, or physically scroll through your Lovable preview environment to verify the visual smoothness myself.

Instead, I can equip you with a rigorous QA (Quality Assurance) checklist to evaluate it exactly like a senior frontend developer would.

Here is how to test the parallax implementation to ensure it meets your brand standards:

1. The Desktop Performance Check
Don't just rely on how it "feels"—measure it.

The FPS Test: Open Google Chrome, press F12 to open Developer Tools, press Ctrl+Shift+P (or Cmd+Shift+P on Mac), type "Rendering", and select "Show Rendering". Check the "Frame Rendering Stats" box. Scroll up and down your hero section. The FPS should stay locked at or near 60 fps. If it drops significantly, the parallax is too heavy.

The Ghosting Check: Scroll at a medium pace and watch the Deep Maroon text over the image. Does the text blur, jitter, or look like it's vibrating against the background? If so, the z-index or rendering approach needs fixing.

2. The Real-Device Mobile Check
Do not rely solely on the browser's mobile emulator for this. Emulators run on your desktop's powerful processor, which hides mobile performance issues.

The Physical Test: Open the live preview link on your actual physical smartphone.

The "Flick" Test: Scroll down rapidly with a quick thumb flick. Does the page freeze for a split second before moving? Does the image snap into place awkwardly?

The Boundary Check: Scroll all the way to the top and slightly pull down (triggering the browser's native "overscroll" or "bounce" effect). Ensure the parallax image doesn't expose a blank white gap above it.

3. The "Clinical Luxury" Visual Audit
Intensity: Does the image translate just slightly (the 5-10% rule we set), or does it move so much that it distracts from your dermo-cosmetics catalog? It should feel like a subtle shift in perspective, not a moving elevator.

Would you like me to prepare a fallback Lovable command that strips out the Framer Motion JavaScript and replaces it with a lighter, CSS-only "fixed background" parallax, just in case the current version fails the mobile performance test?
